Chinese footballer and coach

Xie Hui

**Xie Hui** ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 谢晖; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 謝暉; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Xiè Huī*; born February 14, 1975, in Shanghai) is a Chinese former professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") player and coach.

As a player Xie Hui played as a [forward](/wiki/Forward_(association_football) "Forward (association football)"), representing [Shanghai Shenhua](/wiki/Shanghai_Shenhua "Shanghai Shenhua") throughout the majority of his career, having several spells with them and winning a Chinese league title and [Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/Chinese_FA_Cup "Chinese FA Cup") with the club. He also represented [Chongqing Lifan](/wiki/Chongqing_Lifan "Chongqing Lifan") and German clubs [Alemannia Aachen](/wiki/Alemannia_Aachen "Alemannia Aachen"), [Greuther Fürth](/wiki/SpVgg_Greuther_F%C3%BCrth "SpVgg Greuther Fürth") and [SV Wehen Wiesbaden](/wiki/SV_Wehen_Wiesbaden "SV Wehen Wiesbaden") throughout his career. While internationally he would play in the [1996 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/1996_AFC_Asian_Cup "1996 AFC Asian Cup") and [2000 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2000_AFC_Asian_Cup "2000 AFC Asian Cup").

## Club career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xie_Hui_(footballer)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Club career")]

Xie Hui started his professional football career with [Shanghai Shenhua](/wiki/Shanghai_Shenhua "Shanghai Shenhua") in the 1994 season. After excelling with Shenhua for six seasons in the late 90s where he won one top-tier league title and a [Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/Chinese_FA_Cup "Chinese FA Cup"), Xie Hui transferred to [2nd Bundesliga](/wiki/2._Fu%C3%9Fball-Bundesliga "2. Fußball-Bundesliga") club [Alemannia Aachen](/wiki/Alemannia_Aachen "Alemannia Aachen") in January 2000.[[1]](#cite_note-Xie_Hui-1) During the 2000–01 season, Xie Hui was the top scorer for Aachen with fourteen goals in twenty-four league appearances. The following season Xie Hui netted only once in thirteen appearances and because of this drop in performance, he went back to Shanghai Shenhua on loan in March 2002 to gain more first team experience and to keep his place on the [China national football team](/wiki/China_national_football_team "China national football team"). When his loan finished he transferred to another German second-tier club in [Greuther Fürth](/wiki/SpVgg_Greuther_F%C3%BCrth "SpVgg Greuther Fürth") in August 2002, however after only netting only once in eight league appearances for Greuther Fürth, Xie Hui came back to China to play for [Chongqing Lifan](/wiki/Chongqing_Lifan "Chongqing Lifan") in 2003.

For the third time he rejoined Shenhua in December 2004 on a permanent basis where he once again revived his career and after two years with Shenhua, Xie headed back to Germany when he joined another second tier German club [SV Wehen Wiesbaden](/wiki/SV_Wehen_Wiesbaden "SV Wehen Wiesbaden") on January 1, 2008, on an 18-month contract. When his contract ended Xie would once again return to Shenhua on January 6, 2009, for a testimonial.

## International career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xie_Hui_(footballer)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: International career")]

Xie Hui was China's top scorer during the [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup") qualifying stages, but he was dropped by head coach [Bora Milutinović](/wiki/Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87 "Bora Milutinović") from China's final World Cup squad.

## Managerial career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xie_Hui_(footballer)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Managerial career")]

After the game he officially retired from football and soon became an assistant manager at Shenhua.[[2]](#cite_note-2) He was hired by Shanghai SIPG Football club as an assistant coach.

On August 18, 2021, while Xie Hui was the manager of [Nantong Zhiyun](/wiki/Nantong_Zhiyun_F.C. "Nantong Zhiyun F.C."), a leaked video of him dining with colleagues revealed his controversial comments towards management and coaching staff of other competing teams.[[3]](#cite_note-3) He later was suspended by Nantong Zhiyun.[[4]](#cite_note-4) After apologizing on his comments, he resigned from his manager role at the club.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Dalian Professional

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xie_Hui_(footballer)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Dalian Professional")]

On March 19, 2022, Xie was appointed as the manager of [Dalian Professional](/wiki/Dalian_Professional_F.C. "Dalian Professional F.C.").[[6]](#cite_note-6) During the first year of his management, the team was initially planning to play in the [China League One](/wiki/China_League_One "China League One") (the second level of professional soccer in China) due to the relegation from [Chinese Super League](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League "Chinese Super League") in previous season. However, as [Chongqing Liangjiang Athletic](/wiki/Chongqing_Liangjiang_Athletic_F.C. "Chongqing Liangjiang Athletic F.C.") in the Chinese Super League dissolved before the start of the 2022 season, Dalian Professional was allowed to play in the Chinese Super League as a substitute for Chongqing Liangjiang Athletic for the 2022 season.[[7]](#cite_note-7) On 4 November 2023, Dalian was once again relegated after a 3-2 home defeat against [Shanghai Port](/wiki/Shanghai_Port_F.C. "Shanghai Port F.C.") in the final game of the season, and the club was subsequently disbanded in January 2024.

### Changchun Yatai

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xie_Hui_(footballer)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Changchun Yatai")]

On April 17, 2024, Xie was appointed as the manager of [Chinese Super League](/wiki/Chinese_Super_League "Chinese Super League") club [Changchun Yatai](/wiki/Changchun_Yatai_F.C. "Changchun Yatai F.C.").[[8]](#cite_note-8) On May 7, 2025, Xie resigned due to poor results, with just 1 win in 11 matches.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xie_Hui_(footballer)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Xie's paternal great-grandfather, Xie Ruiying, was a doctor that was sent to Germany to study medicine after [First Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/First_Sino-Japanese_War "First Sino-Japanese War") and briefly worked in England, where Ruiying met Julia Traver, an English nutrition nurse. Xie and Traver got married in 1918 and gave birth to Xie Hui's grandfather, Xie Dihua, who once served as a police officer in [Shanghai International Settlement](/wiki/Shanghai_International_Settlement "Shanghai International Settlement").[[10]](#cite_note-journal-10) Therefore, Xie Hui is of one-eighth English descendant.

On December 9, 2004, Xie married his long-term girlfriend and Chinese supermodel, [Tong Chenjie](/w/index.php?title=Tong_Chenjie&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tong Chenjie (page does not exist)") (佟晨洁), however the couple divorced on June 27, 2011.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Starting from 2018, Xie started dating Dalia, a Russian model who studied for master degrees in China. The two got married on February 14, 2024.[[12]](#cite_note-12) They have two children born prior to the marriage.

## Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Xie_Hui_(footballer)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Honours")]

* [Chinese FA Cup](/wiki/Chinese_FA_Cup "Chinese FA Cup"): 1998
